draft.introduction: Odyssey 9 begins Odysseus’ tale of his wanderings, including the encounter with the Cyclops Polyphemus. The blinding of the Cyclops is the archetype of cunning over brute strength in Western narrative.

translation.text: I said, ‘Cyclops, you ask my name? I will tell you. My name is Nobody. Nobody my mother and father call me.’ And he, pitiless, answered: ‘Nobody will I eat last among your companions.’ When the other Cyclopes asked who harmed him, Polyphemus cried out: ‘Nobody is killing me by cunning!’ And they went away.
translation.original_text: Κύκλωψ εἰρωτᾷς μ’ ὄνομα κλυτόν αὐτὰρ ἐγώ τοι ἐξερέω Οὖτις ἐμοί γ’ ὄνομα

translator_notes.note: The “Nobody” (Οὖτις) trick is the purest expression of Odyssean metis (cunning intelligence). But Odysseus’ later boast of his true name to the blinded Cyclops—motivated by pride—brings Poseidon’s wrath and years of suffering. Intelligence without restraint is self-defeating.
